title: Asset-Side Bank Runs and Liquidity Rationing: A Vicious Cycle
abstract: I analyze runs on credit lines in an infinite-horizon banking model, focusing on the strategic complementarity between bankers and credit line borrowers. Panic drawdowns by borrowers coupled with bank liquidity rationing can mutually reinforce each other, creating a vicious cycle. Using data from U.S. banks, I estimate the model and quantify the amplification effect arising from strategic complementarity. This amplification effect accounted for two-thirds of the contraction of bank credit during the 2008–2009 crisis. Moreover, I show that policies targeting borrowers have a crowding-in effect and can effectively contain credit contraction.

author.Zongbo Huang: School of Management and Economics and Shenzhen Finance Institute, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen 518172, China

article.1. Introduction: The global financial crisis was fueled by panics that arose in unprecedented circumstances. These panics were incited by disruptions in short-term liabilities, such as margin calls and rollover freezes, and by the run-like behavior of asset-side credit line borrowers. Empirical research (Campello et al. 2010,Ivashina and Scharfstein 2010) lends evidence to the credit line run narrative, suggesting that borrowers strategically draw down credit lines as bank liquidity tightens.1 Despite abundant empirical evidence, important questions about the credit line run mechanism still need to be answered. How does it exacerbate credit contraction, and is it quantitatively significant? What are the policy implications? To tackle these questions, I estimate a dynamic banking model using data on U.S. banks. The model encapsulates dynamic interactions between borrowers’ credit line run behavior and bankers’ liquidity rationing, which I refer to asbanker–borrower strategic complementarity. My analysis focuses on the amplification effect due to strategic complementarity rather than on multiple equilibria emphasized in deposit-run models. Using the estimated model, I investigate the quantitative importance of the amplification effect and conduct policy experiments. The amplification effect accounted for two-thirds of the U.S. banks’ credit contraction during the crisis. Moreover, policies targeting borrowers can effectively contain credit contraction and may have a larger effect when combined with policies targeting banks. The strategic complementarity arises from the dual contingency of credit line contracts and the bank balance sheet channel. On the one hand, borrowers can draw down credit lines whenever needed (Kashyap et al. 2002,Nikolov et al. 2019). This contingency makes credit lines similar to deposits and also subject to run. Moreover, credit lines are a form of contingent liquidity provision, as noted bySufi (2009)andAcharya et al. (2021a). Although banks typically honor credit line drawdowns in normal times, banks have the discretion to cut (stop rolling over or revoke) credit lines when banks are in distress and the provision of liquidity becomes costly. This second contingency motivates borrowers to draw down credit lines strategically to avoid credit line cuts. Conversely, as intermediation costs escalate during crises, financing strategic drawdowns becomes costly, which erodes equity and builds up leverage. The balance sheet channel (Bernanke et al. 1999) predicts that bankers will deleverage and ration liquidity, mainly through credit line cuts (Chodorow-Reich and Falato 2022). These two strategic responses, credit line cuts and strategic drawdowns, reinforce each other, creating an amplifying feedback loop. To quantify the importance of this amplification mechanism, I develop an infinite-horizon model inSection 2that captures the banker–borrower strategic complementarity. In the model, a representative banker extends credit lines to a continuum of borrowers who operate projects subject to liquidity shocks in the spirit ofHolmstrom and Tirole (1998). The more borrowers depend on credit lines to ensure against the shocks, the more strongly borrowers react to potential credit line cuts. To capture the bank balance sheet channel, I introduce a capital requirement and a leverage-sensitive intermediation cost. In this setting, a crisis shock reduces bank profits and triggers an amplification spiral between strategic drawdowns and credit line cuts. The extent of this amplification depends on the intensity of strategic complementarities, which in turn is influenced by the borrower’s reliance on credit lines, the banker’s cost sensitivity to leverage, and the cost of cutting credit lines. Notably, because credit line contracts are long term, the strategic complementarity between the banker and the borrower is dynamic in nature and arises from the continuation values. InSection 3, I estimate the model using the simulated method of moments. Estimating models that involve strategic complementarities is challenging because of the potential for simultaneity and omitted variable biases. To address this issue, instead of directly estimating the strategic responses, I draw insights from the social interaction literature (Glaeser et al. 1996), which suggests that the stronger the strategic interactions, the larger the amplification effect. Consequently, I compare credit line drawdowns and credit growth at the bank level before and during the 2008–2009 crisis to gauge the strength of strategic complementarity. The estimated model exhibits a good in-sample fit, replicates the time-series empirical patterns, and generates acyclical leverage, as banks can deleverage by cutting credit lines rather than resorting to firesales. Notably, although I do not target any cross-sectional moment in the estimation, the estimated model accurately captures the empirical associations between credit contraction and precrisis leverage. Next, I use the estimated model to quantify the amplification mechanism inSection 4. I begin by plotting the impulse response functions to a shift from normal times to crises and show that dampening the response of either the bankers or borrowers reduces the amplification effect. InSection 4.2, I study a counterfactual that removes strategic drawdowns, thereby eliminating the banker–borrower strategic complementarity. I then quantify the amplification effect by comparing this scenario with the estimated model. The results indicate that the amplification effect accounts for two-thirds of the credit contraction in the estimated model. Specifically, bank credit contracts by 1.54% per quarter in the estimated model, whereas in the counterfactual without amplification, it contract only by 0.54%. To assess the robustness of the quantitative result, I compute the sensitivities to estimation moments inSection 4.3, followingAndrews et al. (2017), and find a limited misspecification bias. Finally, I discuss policy implications inSection 5. Although ex ante prudential policies, such as capital requirements, do not directly target credit lines, the policies can effectively reduce credit contraction by enhancing bank lending capacity during crises. Specifically, I consider a set of countercyclical policies that fix the capital requirement in crises and increase it from 6%, the baseline, to 6.4% in normal times. In the counterfactual with a requirement of 6.4%, both credit contraction and strategic drawdowns reduce by approximately half. I then investigate two ex post policies: the funding for lending (FFL) scheme, which aims to reduce bank intermediation costs, and the corporate credit facilities (CCFs), which provide direct liquidity to borrowers. Although policies like FFL have traditionally been used to support bank credit to the real economy, policies like CCFs, which target real businesses, have gained attention and were implemented during the COVID-19 crisis due to concerns about potential bank malfunction. My analysis unveils a compelling argument supporting policies like CCFs that target borrowers. Although not directly targeting banks, such policies can effectively contain credit contraction and stimulate bank credit due to the banker–borrower strategic complementarity. Furthermore, the impact of such policies may be even greater when combined with FFL schemes. My paper contributes to several strands of literature on banking and finance. First, it builds upon the vast bank run literature (Allen and Gale 1998,Cooper and Ross 1998,Rochet and Vives 2004,Goldstein and Pauzner 2005,Vives 2005). In particular, previous studies have highlighted the potential negative consequence of liquidity management strategies, such as suspension of deposit convertibility (Engineer 1989,Ennis and Keister 2009,Cipriani et al. 2014) and fund cash-rebuilding (Morris et al. 2017,Zeng 2017,Shek et al. 2018), within a dynamic framework of bank runs. However, these studies have primarily focused on creditors’ reactions to liquidity adjustments. In contrast, my paper extends these works by incorporating insights from the bank balance sheet channel and studying the strategic complementarity between bankers and borrowers. My paper also relates to the literature on how capacity constraints lead to strategic complementarities (Bond and Rai 2009,Arellano and Kocherlakota 2014,Drozd and Serrano-Padial 2018,Infante and Vardoulakis 2021). Unlike these works, the constraint in my model arises endogenously from the banker’s dynamic problem. More broadly, my paper explores the feedback loop between increased liquidity demand (runs) and reduced liquidity provision (credit line cuts), thereby connecting to research on systemic bank runs (Brunnermeier 2009).Liu (2016)study the interplay between creditor runs and liquidity provision in the interbank market.Eisenbach (2017)andLiu (2023)emphasize the feedback loop between creditor runs and the liquidation values of banks’ assets in a common market. In contrast, the reduced liquidity provision in my model arises from the banker’s dynamic deleveraging motives rather than cross-bank liquidity crunch. My paper also complements prior research on dynamic coordination failures (Cheng and Milbradt 2012,He and Xiong 2012,Schroth et al. 2014) by showing how dynamic strategic complementarities arise from contingent credit lines rather than a staggered debt structure. Second, my paper contributes to the macro-finance literature on financial frictions. Prior works such asGertler and Kiyotaki (2015)andGertler et al. (2020)have established a link between the bank balance sheet channel and bank runs (Miao and Wang 2015,Benhabib et al. 2016). These works characterize runs as self-fulfilling rollover freezes, as outlined inCole and Kehoe (2000). In contrast, my model attributes runs to liquidity mismatch in the spirit ofDiamond and Dybvig (1983). My model also features realistic partial runs, which closely align with empirical evidence, as opposed to a sudden and complete banking sector collapse. Finally, my paper builds on the empirical literature on credit lines (Chava and Roberts 2008,Nini et al. 2009,Roberts and Sufi 2009a,Sufi 2009).Ivashina and Scharfstein (2010)find that borrowers run on credit lines out of concern that distressed banks may cut their credit lines.Ippolito et al. (2016)illustrate that an exogenous shock to the Italian interbank market triggers correlated creditors’ runs on the liability side and credit line runs on the asset side of banks’ balance sheet. Their findings suggest a potential interplay between forced termination of credit line contracts upon bank failure and credit line runs. Furthermore,Acharya et al. (2020)andChodorow-Reich and Falato (2022)highlight that credit lines, acting as a form of contingent liquidity supply, depend on the soundness of the issuing banks. Drawing on these empirical studies, I construct a dynamic banking model that depicts how credit line runs can significantly impact bank fundamentals and subsequently affect banks’ contingent supply of credit lines through the bank balance sheet channel. Moreover, I estimate this model featuring the strategic interaction between credit line runs and contingent liquidity supply, quantify its amplification effect and conduct policy experiments.
article.2. Infinite Horizon Model: In this section, I develop an infinite-horizon banking model with credit lines. The model features a representative banker who lends to a continuum of credit line and passive term loan borrowers. The model incorporates three key ingredients to capture the strategic complementarity between the banker and the credit line borrowers. First, borrowers may need liquidity every period and obtain it by drawing down credit lines. This setup is drawn from the work ofHolmstrom and Tirole (1998). Second, the banker has the discretion to cut unused credit lines. This is supported by empirical evidence that demonstrates the contingency of credit lines on borrowers’ characteristics and macroeconomic fluctuations, as documented bySufi (2009)andRoberts and Sufi (2009a). During the 2008–2009 financial crisis, banks, despite the difficulty of forcing repayment of drawn-down credit lines, can and do exercise this discretion based on their own soundness (Acharya et al. 2020,Chodorow-Reich and Falato 2022).2Third, the banker’s marginal value from cutting credit lines increases with bank leverage. This is because the capital requirement is more likely to become binding in the future, and the banker faces intermediation costs that rise with leverage, which is in line with empirical evidence as corroborated byBerger et al. (2017)andGambacorta and Shin (2018). The combination of the capital requirement and leverage-sensitive costs allows the model to quantitatively match the leverage dynamics observed in the data. The banker-borrower strategic complementarity arises because borrowers are concerned about the banker cutting their credit lines. Therefore, the borrowers have an incentive to draw down credit lines strategically, even without an immediate liquidity need. More credit line cuts intensify this concern among borrowers and lead to an increase in strategic drawdowns. The increase in strategic drawdowns that force the banker to lend results in an increase in bank assets and a decrease in equity when the bank’s profit margin turns negative, a situation likely during crises. Thus, bank leverage increases, as well as the banker’s marginal value of cutting credit lines, leading to more aggressive cuts. Therefore, the strategic drawdowns and credit line cuts reinforce each other, amplifying shocks that could impact either the banker or the borrowers. It is important to note that this analysis focuses on the amplification due to strategic complementarity rather than multiple equilibria and associated bank failures. Although no bank failed due to credit line drawdowns during the 2008–2009 financial crisis, runs on credit lines can still occur and significantly amplify credit contractions. 2.1. Banker’s Problem In each period, the banker begins with equityE, term loansLextended to firms outside the model, and credit lines with a total limitΦΦ. The banker raises deposits to finance both term loans and used credit lines with state-dependent loan ratesr(s) and deposit ratesz(s). The banker can ration liquidity through credit line cuts.4Importantly, the liquidity rationing strategy cannot depend on borrowers’ idiosyncratic shocks that the banker does not observe. This implies that the banker faces ex ante identical borrowers and is indifferent to cutting the credit lines of any two borrowers. Consequently, the banker’s strategy degenerates to the total size of credit limit cuts Δ, which is limited to the size of unused credit linesΦ−𝐿𝐷⁡(𝐒)Φ−LD⁡(S). In addition, the banker can issue new credit in the form of credit lines or term loans, denoted asNCandNL, respectively; however, because the model focuses on situations where the banker rations liquidity, the choice between credit lines and term loans does not significantly impact the model’s implications. Specifically, I assume the banker has a predetermined target ratio between credit lines and term loans, where credit lines account for 56% of bank credit and term loans account for 44%. If credit line cuts result in a ratio below the target, the banker will issue new credit lines to restore the ratio to the target level. Otherwise, if the ratio is at the target level, the banker will issue new credit lines and term loans proportionally to maintain the target ratio.5 Moreover, to ensure a well-defined bank problem, I assume that the banker exits with a probability ofξbin each period.6When the banker exits, the banker sells the bank and distributes equity𝐸′E′as dividends. This assumption is motivated by the prevalent pattern of mergers and acquisitions within the U.S. banking industry before 2000. This trend, largely driven by the easing of geographic restrictions, continued into the 2000s. On average, mergers and acquisitions account for the exit of approximately 1.6% of banks in the data each quarter. To streamline the model, I assume the bank is sold to a new entrant rather than an existing banker. The banker may also pay out dividendsdbefore exiting, but I do not allow them to issue new equity because banks issued very little equity during the 2008–2009 crisis. The banker’s problem becomes This worst-case approach is motivated by the common banking practice of maintaining a buffer above the regulatory requirements to avoid corrective actions and ensure sufficient equity, even in extreme situations. It can also be explained through a self-fulfilling mechanism. Consider a situation where the capital requirement is met if only a fraction of borrowers draw down their credit lines but is violated if all borrowers choose to do so. To avoid the unfavorable situation where all other borrowers draw down their credit lines and the requirement is violated, each borrower might rationally opt to draw down credit lines preemptively. This results in a violation of the capital requirement, confirming borrowers’ precautionary concern and justifies the reasoning behind considering the worst-case scenario.7 As a robustness check, I consider an alternative, best-case scenario where only borrowers with liquidity needs would draw down credit lines. I then re-estimate the model, as outlined in Section D of the Online Appendix. The results of this re-estimation remain qualitatively consistent and quantitatively akin to the baseline model, suggesting that the model is resilient under alternative capital requirements that likely range between these two extremes. For instance, under Basel I, the unused portion of long-term credit lines are included in the risk-weighted assets with a conversion factor of 50%. Thus, the banker opts for immediate credit line cuts until the equity-to-credit ratio attains the optimal level, where the marginal value of credit is zero. When considering the issuance of new credit, the banker encounters a similar tradeoff between the adjustment cost and the benefit of leveraging up to achieve the optimal equity-to-credit ratio: 2.4. Equilibrium and the Amplification Mechanism Banks adjust both prices and quantities in practice to reduce their exposure to credit lines. Both price adjustments or quantity adjustments create the same incentives for borrowers to strategically draw down their credit lines, which can increase their bargaining power and avoid the adjustments. Therefore, strategic complementarity is not tied to any particular type of adjustment. Nevertheless, because of the information asymmetry in the financial market (Stiglitz and Weiss 1981) and to ensure tractability, I focus on quantity adjustments and analyze pure liquidity rationing. In this scenario, the banker randomly restricts the unused credit lines of borrowers that are identical ex ante, and each borrower faces the same likelihood of separation. The probability of separation, represented byp, equals the size of credit line cuts (Δ) divided by the total unused credit lines (Φ−𝐿𝐷Φ−LD). Importantly, because the borrower’s value function is linear, random separation is equivalent to a reduction in the borrower’s credit limit. I consider the Markov perfect equilibrium, where value functions, the banker’s policy function, and aggregated drawdowns are functions of the payoff-relevant states𝐒=[𝐸,Φ,𝐿,𝑠]S=[E,Φ,L,s]. A Markov perfect equilibrium is given by value functions for borrowers and the banker {v, V}, policy functions {c, k, l}, and{𝑑,Δ,𝑁𝐶,𝑁𝐿}{d,Δ,NC,NL}, and probabilitypsuch that Value functions {v, V} solve Problems (7) and (1)–(4) given the policy functions; Policy functions {c, k, l} are optimal given value function v and the banker’s policy functions; Policy functions{𝑑,Δ,𝑁𝐿,𝑁𝐶}{d,Δ,NL,NC}are optimal for the banker given value function V and the drawdown policy; The total credit limit of all borrowers equals the credit limit granted by the bankerΦΦ. The model integrates a dynamic strategic complementarity between borrowers’ decisions to draw down on their credit lines and the banker’s liquidity rationing. An integral implication of this strategic complementarity is the amplification of adverse shocks through a feedback loop. Thus, this model delves into the consequential amplification of an adverse shock that afflicts banks, shedding light on the 2008–2009 financial crisis. In the model, the banker’s profit declines sharply during crises due to state-dependent loan rates, deposit rates, and operating costs. Consequently, at the onset of a crisis shock, the banker has incentives to ration liquidity and increase the equity-to-credit ratio to a much higher optimal target. As the primary margin of liquidity rationing, credit line cuts trigger the feedback loop, which unfolds as follows. Anticipating a potential separation from their existing credit lines, borrowers are incentivized to draw down their credit lines in advance strategically. The sensitivity of these strategic drawdowns to the liquidity rationing policy is determined by the probability of substitution,η. The higher this probability, the more easily borrowers can find an alternative source of liquidity, leading to a reduced magnitude of strategic drawdowns. An upsurge in aggregate drawdowns consequently leads to an increase in bank leverage. Drawn credit lines show up as loans, which mechanically boost bank assets. Concurrently, bank equity reduces as the bank’s profit margin may turn negative during crises, and credit line drawdowns force the banker to lend. Given the capital requirements and the leverage-sensitive operating cost, the increase in leverage drives the banker to deleverage through more extensive credit line cuts. The extent to which the banker’s liquidity rationing policy responds to aggregate drawdowns depends on the sensitivity of the operating cost to leverage,ψ. If the operating cost per loan increases more with leverage, the liquidity rationing policy will respond more strongly. In summary, the initial crisis shock triggers the first round of credit line cuts, which then leads to an increase in strategic drawdowns; this is followed by a second round of credit line cuts. This feedback process continues to repeat, creating a vicious cycle that magnifies the impact of the crisis shock. It is worth noting that shocks on the borrowers’ side, such as the heightened liquidity demand during the COVID-19 crisis, can also set off a similar feedback loop. The amplification mechanism depends on both information frictions and contractual frictions embedded in the model. Beyond the information friction discussed above that leads to liquidity rationing, a key friction is the inability of either the banker or other borrowers to discern whether a borrower needs liquidity. Consequently, the banker cannot prevent the borrowers from drawing down strategically, and borrowers cannot insure each other against liquidity needs. Two substantial contractual frictions are also present. First, it is difficult to enforce repayment once credit lines are drawn. Therefore, the banker can only cut unused credit lines, which creates an incentive for strategic drawdowns. Second, the banker cannot commit to a liquidity rationing policy, as regulations prohibit credit line contracts from being contingent on bank balance sheet strength. This results in a time-inconsistency problem. From the ex ante perspective, the banker prefers to cut fewer credit lines to contain the credit line run. However, after observing the aggregate drawdown, the banker’s future self would respond optimally ex post rather than sticking to the committed policy.Section 4investigates two counterfactuals where either friction is removed to quantify the amplification mechanism. 2.5. Numerical Solution I solve the model numerically using global methods. I briefly sketch the algorithm below and discuss the implementation details in Section C of the Online Appendix. To approximate value and policy functions, I use piecewise linear functions evaluated at a state vector grid and iterate the functions until converge. To update each agent’s optimal policies and value functions in each round, I follow the algorithm proposed byPakes and McGuire (1994)and the approximated value functions and the other agents’ policy functions from the previous iteration. A key challenge in solving the model is the kinks in value and policy functions, which arise due to borrowers’ drawdown decisions responding only to the banker’s strategy when the banker rations liquidity. These kinks propagate through strategic interactions to the entire state space and substantially complicate the solution. To deal with this issue, I followIskhakov et al. (2017)and introduce random noise, which can be a structural uncertainty shock or a smoothing device. I then use simple moving averages to smooth out the kinks and ensure consistent solutions under a set of parameters in the estimation. Another challenge is that strategic complementarity may lead to multiple equilibria. However, because the data do not suggest clustering into a small finite number of distributions, as implied by multiple equilibria, I focus on the amplification effect and partial runs instead. To avoid the issue of multiplicity, I impose an upper bound on the degree of strategic complementarity during the estimation process. After estimating the model, I verify numerically that the equilibrium is unique using different initial guesses of the value and policy functions. 2.6. Model Discussion The amplification mechanism in the model is robust to alternative modeling choices. First, the assumption that borrowers can draw down credit lines prior to the banker’s move is based on the observation that banks renegotiate credit lines with delays due to information frictions. However, this mechanism remains valid under different assumptions. Specifically, credit line runs can still transpire even if the banker acts first, as long as credit line cuts are implemented gradually, as suggested by the data. More broadly, even though I rely on empirical evidence to motivate the features of credit line contracts, these features can also arise as an optimal design in the presence of realistic contractual and informational frictions (Payne 2018). Second, although the borrower side is stylized in this model, the linearity of the value function allows for the introduction of more complex heterogeneities. For instance, one could incorporate the covenant violation status and assume that the banker can only cut credit lines that have violations. This would allow for varied borrower responses based on their violation status. Similarly, although the model doesn’t differentiate between retirement and revocation, it is conceivable that borrowers respond less to retirement as banks have more bargaining power over maturing credit lines. The model can accommodate such borrower heterogeneities and other idiosyncratic shocks that occur after drawdowns. What matters for aggregate dynamics is the average response. For aggregate dynamics, estimating the response of aggregate drawdowns to the separation probability is sufficient, as it reflects a weighted average of individual responses. Moreover, the model can incorporate autocorrelated shocks and shocks realized before drawdowns by introducing additional state variables and expanding the strategic space. Finally, it is worth noting that the model is a partial equilibrium model. Although loan and deposit rates depend on the exogenous state that captures monetary policies outside the model, the rates do not depend on endogenous state variables. However, it is possible to endogenize the deposit rates and microfound the operating costs by incorporating an elastic deposit supply. Additionally, loan rates can also be recognized as a spread that depends only on the exogenous state, above an endogenous base rate. In contrast, endogenizing loan rates for each credit line is not tractable as it would significantly expand the state and strategy space. This would complicate the banker’s problem; for example, the liquidity rationing policy would depend on the endogenous rate and its distribution across borrowers. Besides prices, the model can be extended to include other general equilibrium features. For instance, one possible extension is to endogenize the probability of substitution, making it dependent on the aggregate liquidity provision and search frictions. This extension introduces another externality among identical banks, leading to further amplification. However, estimating the search process would require additional data. Another potential extension is to model where funds flow after being drawn down strategically. If more funds flow back to the banking system, the intermediation cost will decrease, attenuating the amplification mechanism. Nevertheless, this force is likely to be small in magnitude. During the 2008–2009 crisis, borrowers tended to invest funds in assets with an explicit government guarantee, such as Treasury bonds (Acharya and Mora 2015). Although some funds may flow back to banks, the funds are more likely to go through the wholesale market, which incurs higher costs (Drechsler et al. 2021).

Estimation: Estimating models that involve strategic complementarities presents a challenge because of the endogenous nature of agents’ strategies and the potential impact of common shocks. To address this issue, I adopt an approach from the social interaction literature and estimate the model at the group (bank) level rather than the individual (loan) level. Specifically, I draw on the insights ofGlaeser et al. (1996). The authors argue that the high variance in crime rates across time and space, such as the significant decline in U.S. homicide rates from 1933 to 1961, cannot be fully explained by observable factors, suggesting the importance of strategic interactions. Similarly, I examine the time-series variance in bank credit growth and credit line drawdowns before and during the 2008–2009 crisis. As banks did not ration liquidity before the crisis, the strategic complementarity between bankers and borrowers only emerged during the crisis. Thus, contrasting these periods reveals the degree of strategic complementarity. Specifically, a stronger strategic complementarity leads to more severe credit contractions and aggressive credit line drawdowns. One potential concern is that factors other than the strategic complementarity between banks and borrowers may contribute to the observed time series variation. For example, an exogenous shock that impacts banks during the crisis could also lead to a contraction in bank credit. Additionally, other amplification mechanisms, such as creditors’ runs and the collateral channel, may be at play, potentially contaminating the estimation results. To address this concern, I control observed differences in loan rates, deposit rates, and net noninterest expenses across time. By doing so, I account for any shocks and mechanisms that operate through these rates and costs. This approach ensures that any remaining time series variation can be attributed to the strategic complementarity between banks and borrowers. It is important to note that in counterfactual analysis, where we cannot directly observe the time series changes in these rates and costs, additional caution is required when interpreting the results. Another similar concern is the increase in credit line drawdowns during the crisis may reflect higher demand for operating liquidity rather than strategic interactions. However, several pieces of evidence suggest that this alternative explanation is unlikely. First, according to the Federal Reserve’s Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices, banks reported a broad reduction, rather than an increase, in demand for new loans during the crisis. Second, the loan-to-credit ratio barely increases for banks with positive credit growth, which suggests that borrowers with these banks do not draw down more. Moreover, the empirical literature finds little assortative matching between banks and borrowers (Chodorow-Reich and Falato 2022). Thus, the aggregate demand for operating liquidity is likely similar for all banks, which cannot account for the observed increase in drawdowns. Third,Chodorow-Reich and Falato (2022)show that only borrowers who violate credit line covenants and borrow from less healthy banks used a larger fraction of credit lines in 2008–2009 than in 2006–2007. This finding is consistent with the credit line runs narrative rather than a higher demand for operating liquidity. To further address this concern, I conduct a robustness analysis in Section D of the Online Appendix by re-estimating the model with different assumptions about the demand for operating liquidity during crises. The outcomes of this analysis suggest that the magnitudes of the amplification effect remain consistent, thereby supporting the hypothesis that strategic complementarity is the main driver of the observed bank-level outcomes. 3.1. Model Fit The model reproduces the empirical moments used in the estimation. Panel B ofTable 4compares the empirical and model-implied moments. Their differences are statistically insignificant. In the data and the model, bank credit contracts significantly during crisis periods at a rate of approximately 6% per year. Moreover, the asset-to-credit ratio increases from 0.748 in normal times to 0.772 in crises, as borrowers draw down more credit lines. Tobin’s Q in the simulated data are slightly smaller than the actual data, possibly because of the imprecise measurement of banks’ market values in the actual data. The model replicates the time series patterns discussed inSection 3.1. This is unsurprising because I target the credit growth and asset-to-credit ratios in the estimation. The model also generates acyclical leverage: leverage increases slightly from 10.0 before the crisis to 10.4. The weak association between asset growth and book equity growth is consistent with the empirical evidence documented byAdrian and Shin (2011). Although leverage is countercyclical in most macro-finance models, contingent credit lines allow banks to shrink their balance sheets less costly and more promptly. As a result, leverage does not increase significantly in crises. As an external validity check, I examine a conditional moment that is directly connected to the policy functions in the model but not used in the estimation. Specifically, I consider the coefficient that associates credit contraction during the 2008–2009 crisis with bank leverage immediately before the crisis. Because the model has limited cross-sectional variation, I start from the normal steady state, perturb the equity-to-credit ratio, and simulate responses of credit growth with different equity-to-credit ratios. I then compare the regression coefficient with the following specification in actual and simulated data. The coefficient is expected to be positive; lower leverage before a crisis should result in less deleveraging pressure, leading to a smaller credit contraction. The coefficient, derived from actual data, is 0.280 with a standard error of 0.117, whereas the coefficient from simulated data are 0.346. Notably, even if this conditional moment is not explicitly targeted during the estimation process, the estimated model effectively captures the cross-sectional correlations present in the actual data. This finding aligns withChodorow-Reich and Falato (2022), who show that banks in worse health are more likely to cut credit lines following a violation. Beyond the prediction on credit growth, banks with lower precrisis leverage should also experience fewer credit line drawdowns, in line with the empirical findings inIppolito et al. (2016). The estimated model can also generate realistic cross-sectional variations driven by differences in the other state variables, for instance, the loan-to-credit ratio. In the model, banks with more loans and fewer credit lines are less exposed to strategic drawdowns, leading to a weaker amplification effect. Consistently,Acharya and Mora (2015)find that banks with more loans precrisis cut back fewer new credit issuance, andAcharya et al. (2021b)show that stock prices of banks with fewer undrawn credit lines declined less.

Amplification of Strategic Complement: In this section, I present plots of impulse responses to illustrate the amplification mechanism and quantify the amplification effect due to the banker-borrower strategic complementarity. 4.1. Impulse Response Functions Figure 2plots the impulse responses resulting from a shift from the normal steady state to the crisis state. During a crisis, the intermediation cost hikes, and the banker targets a much lower capital and rations liquidity by cutting credit lines. As a result, bank credit (the upper panel) drops by 1.5% in the first quarter. The cumulative credit contraction amounts to 6.0% during the first year and 11.7% during the first two years. The right panel plots the impulse response of the borrowers. As the banker cuts credit lines, the borrowers face a threat of separation. The borrower respond by drawing down credit lines strategically, with approximately 8% of those who do not need liquidity still drawing down their credit lines to secure them. The responses of the banker and the borrowers reinforce each other. Therefore, dampening the response of either side can restrict the amplification effect. To illustrate this point,Figure 2includes two counterfactual impulse responses: a case with a higher probability of substitution (𝜂=0.35η=0.35) and one with a higher rationing cost (𝛾−=0.13γ−=0.13). A higherηmakes borrowers less reliant on credit lines and respond less to credit line cuts. Moreover, when bankers face a higher rationing cost, the bankers respond less to drawdowns and ration liquidity more gradually. In either case, the amplification effect is contained, resulting in less severe credit contraction and fewer strategic drawdowns. 4.2. Quantifying the Amplification Effect This section studies two counterfactuals to quantify the amplification effect. First, I consider a model variation in which the banker can commit to his strategy. Enabling the banker to commit resolves the time-inconsistent problem. Specifically, I adjust the model’s timing to allow the banker to announce liquidity provisions, such as credit line cuts or new credit issuance, at the beginning of each period and then adhere to the announcement. In this counterfactual with commitment, the banker recognizes that aggregate drawdown will escalate if the banker commits to more credit line cuts. As a result, the banker internalizes this negative consequence and becomes more reluctant to do so. Table 5compares the counterfactual against the estimated baseline model. With commitment, the banker largely refrains from rationing liquidity unless the capital requirement is binding. As a result, in the first year of a crisis, the banker chooses not to cut credit lines at all, resulting in zero credit contraction. Consequently, borrowers choose not to make strategic drawdowns. Moreover, both the banker and the borrowers become better off. The borrowers benefit because bank commitment makes their credit lines more secure. At the same time, the banker’s welfare improves because the banker can at least commit to the equilibrium strategy in the baseline model without commitment. Consistently, Tobin’s Q (the sum of bank valueVand liabilities divided by assets) and the value of credit lines (𝑣2⁡(𝐒)v2⁡(S)in the model) in normal times become higher than in the baseline model. One caveat of the quantitative analysis is that it does not factor in other potentially important amplification mechanisms, such as creditors’ runs and the collateral channel. The Lucas critique is relevant in this context. Although our model does account for these alternative mechanisms in a reduced-form manner, through state-dependent interest rates and costs, these mechanisms may react once the strategic complementarity is nullified in the counterfactual scenario. This could lead to changes in related parameters. Although integrating all alternative mechanisms into a single framework is beyond the scope of this paper, it remains valuable to address the potential biases that may result from their exclusion. Specifically, given that these alternative mechanisms and the strategic complementarity are likely to reinforce each other, the alternative mechanisms are inherently contained when the strategic complementarity is eliminated. This could lead the counterfactual analysis to underestimate the amplification effect. Thus, despite the difficulty in fully addressing this issue, the quantified amplification effect detailed in this paper should be considered a lower bound. This underscores the substantial role of strategic complementarity between the banker and the borrowers. Strategic Interactions Among Borrowers Although my analysis focuses on the banker–borrower strategic complementarity, strategic interactions among borrowers are also at play. In this section, I decompose the separation probability to show that strategic interactions among borrowers have a limited impact. Specifically, I rewrite the separation probability as follows: The first term on the right-hand side, the credit line cuts divided by total unused credit lines, comes directly from liquidity rationing. It captures the separation probability a single borrower faces, assuming all other borrowers choose not to draw down their credit lines strategically. The second term accounts for borrowers’ strategic interactions. When other borrowers draw down their credit lines strategically, each borrower faces a higher separation probability because the size of the unused credit lines reduces. With a fixed Δ, the second term increases with the number of borrowers who make strategic drawdowns𝐿𝐷−ΛLD−Λ. In other words, as more borrowers run, borrowers who have not yet drawn down their credit lines are subject to a higher separation probability. Using the estimated model, I compute the two terms and find that 92.3% of the separation probability comes from the first term, whereas only 7.7% comes from the second term. This indicates that the strategic interactions among borrowers only play a secondary role in the amplification mechanism.
article.5. Policy Implications: This section uses the estimated model to evaluate ex ante financial regulations and ex post policies aimed at supporting bank lending during crises. Prudential regulations, such as the capital requirement, enhance bank lending capacity, whereas ex post fiscal and monetary policies, such as FFL schemes and CCFs, incentivizes banks to lend to borrowers. 5.1. Capital Requirements I first examine the impact of prudential regulations that impose tighter capital requirements. The capital requirement requires that the equity-to-asset must exceed a certain threshold. As discussed above, I consider a worst-case scenario where all credit lines may be drawn down and set the threshold to 6% according to regulation before the 2008–2009 financial crisis. The financial accelerator literature has demonstrated that quantity requirements can address the distortions caused by pecuniary externalities. Likewise, in my model, the banker fails to internalize the impact of liquidity rationing on borrowers’ drawdown decisions, leading to excessive leverage. Therefore, a tighter capital requirement can induce the banker to maintain lower leverage in normal times, helping to dampen the vicious cycle by keeping more dry powder available. Furthermore, an effective capital requirement should be countercyclical, inducing lower leverage without imposing additional pressure on banks during crises (Davydiuk 2017,Malherbe 2020). I implement changes in capital requirements as follows. First, I use the equity-to-credit ratio in the model as the capital. Second, I keep the threshold of 6% in crises and adjust the threshold in normal periods (from 6% to 6.1%, 6.2%, 6.3%, and 6.4%, respectively). Finally, I solve the model with various capital requirements and compare the results for credit contraction and strategic drawdowns. It is worth noting that the steady states differ in those counterfactuals. Under tighter capital requirements, banks feature lower leverage in the normal steady states. Panel B inTable 5presents the results. A tighter requirement is effective. Compared with the baseline model, an increase in the threshold from 6% to 6.4% reduces credit contraction and strategic drawdowns by approximately half. Moreover, because a tighter requirement also reduces credit growth in normal times, its effect on Tobin’s Q of normal periods is not monotonic. Additionally, conditional on having a credit line, the value of the line increases as the requirement tightens and banks cut fewer credit lines. Therefore, as a crucial component of the Basel framework, the capital requirement serves not only to mitigate risk-shifting behavior and the “too big to fail” risk but can also help to contain the amplification caused by credit line runs. Additionally, the capital requirement provides a simpler and more transparent means of regulating credit lines compared with directly regulating credit lines themselves, which can be complex and difficult to manage. 5.2. FFL and CCF Next, I examine ex post policies to support bank lending: FFL schemes and CCFs. FFL schemes are widely used to provide funds at cheaper rates than the market. For example, the Bank of England and the Eurosystem adopted such schemes during the Global Financial Crisis. The schemes share with asset purchase programs, such as quantitative easing, the same goal of reducing banks’ intermediation costs. I implement the FFL schemes as a reduction in the intermediation cost parameteracin the model. Different from FFL schemes, CCFs provide liquidity to corporations directly. There is a longstanding concern that whether banks can effectively channel funds to real economies; in particular, banks may hoard liquidity due to precautionary motives, or banks may channel funds only to large borrowers but not small and medium businesses. Therefore, policymakers have called on alternative policies to support corporations directly. For example, during the recent Covid crisis, major economies implemented CCFs to support large firms and small and medium businesses. I implement the CCFs in the model as an increase in the probability of substitutionηafter borrowers’ credit lines are cut. This paper provides a new argument for direct support to corporations. Given the banker–borrower strategic complementarity, even if only the banker is hit directly during crises, policies targeting borrowers can be effective in boosting bank credit supply. Suppose borrowers can get access to alternative liquidity, such as CCFs. The marginal benefit of strategic drawdowns reduces. Thus, the total drawdown decreases, the banker faces less pressure to deleverage and cuts fewer credit lines. To quantify this crowding-in effect of CCFs, I simulate the policy counterfactuals in two steps. First, I solve the model with the estimated parameters adjusted for police experiments. Specifically, I increase the probability of substitutionηby 0.5%–4% (from 31.7% in the benchmark model). From a back-of-envelope calculation, a one percentage point increase corresponds to approximately 10 billion dollars of direct liquidity provision, comparable to the size of CCFs adopted in the COVID crisis.11In the second step, I calculate the impulse responses to a shift from the normal steady state in the estimated model (with estimated parameters and no policy adjustment) to the crisis state. Starting from the same steady state captures the ex post nature of the policies and allows a close comparison. I also assume that policymakers are committed to the policies until the crisis ends and in future crises. Figure 3displays the reductions in credit contraction in counterfactuals relative to the baseline model (the dashed line with circles). For example, if the probability of substitution increases by 4% (from 31.7% to 35.7%), quarterly credit contraction will decrease from 1.54% in the baseline model to 1.3%. Furthermore, I consider a second set of counterfactuals implementing FFL to gauge the economic significance of the crowding-in effect. Specifically, I reduce the intermediation costacby 0.2–1.6 basis points.12The dotted line with triangles inFigure 3plots the reductions in credit contraction in this second set of counterfactuals. The results show that a 0.04 increase in the probability of substitution and a 1.6 basis-point reduction in the intermediation cost have similar effects in boosting credit growth in crises. It is tempting to further compare the effects per dollar spent in CCFs versus FFL schemes, yet the two policies are not comparable. CCFs work through loan quantities, whereas FFL schemes focus on intermediation costs (prices) and provide a subsidy below the market. A related debate is whether to adopt both policies simultaneously. Given that both are effective in spurring credit growth, it seems that the marginal contribution of either policy will diminish when the other policy is present, which does not support simultaneous adoption. In contrast, it turns out that the combined effects (the solid line with diamonds inFigure 3) can be larger than the sum of the two individual effects. Across all comparisons, the combined effect ranges from 0.65 to 1.4 times the sum of the two individual effects. In particular, in the counterfactual where the cost reduces by 1.2 basis points and the probability of substitution increases by 3%, the combined effect (0.33% reduction in credit contraction) is larger than the sum of two individual effects (0.11% for CCFs and 0.12% for FFL schemes). This intriguing result justifies the adoption of CCFs and FFL schemes simultaneously. Intuitively, it is because the banker’ and the borrowers’ strategies are nonlinear. Implementing either policy can contain the amplification, leading to fewer credit line cuts and strategic drawdowns. Meanwhile, the degree of complementarity may change in either direction because of the nonlinear strategies. If the complementarity strengthens, the second policy will introduce a larger marginal effect when the first policy is present than absent.
article.6. Conclusion: Asset-side bank runs can occur even without any imminent threat of bank failure. The asset-side runs are not unique to the 2008–2009 financial crisis; a similar phenomenon was also prominent during the COVID-19 crisis. To demonstrate this run mechanism, I have developed a dynamic model that integrates the bank balance sheet channel with credit line runs. A novel banker–borrower strategic complementarity arises where liquidity rationing in the form of credit line cuts induces strategic drawdowns, leading to deleveraging pressure and further liquidity rationing. This process repeats and forms a vicious cycle that amplifies adverse shocks during crises. I have estimated the model using data on large U.S. banks before and during the 2008–2009 crisis. Counterfactual experiments reveal that the amplification effect accounts for two-thirds of the credit contraction, approximately 1% shortfalls quarterly in the first year into a crisis. I have also used the estimated model to study policy experiments. Ex ante prudential policies, such as countercyclical capital requirements, enhance bank lending capacity during crises. In addition, I have analyzed policies that reduce bank intermediation costs and those providing direct liquidity to corporations. Because of the strategic complementarity, the latter policies can effectively contain credit contraction even if these policies do not directly affect banks. The quantitative significance of the amplification mechanism raises several important questions for future research. First, it is important to investigate ways to improve the design of credit line contracts. My results suggest that allowing banks to commit to credit lines can mitigate the amplification effect. However, how to achieve this warrants further scrutiny. Intriguingly, credit lines with less strict covenants and split controls have become popular since the 2008–2009 crisis (Berlin et al. 2020). Second, exploring how strategic complementarity affects aggregate investment and output would be valuable. To achieve this, a complete characterization of credit line borrowers would be necessary while keeping the analysis tractable. It is also important to consider liquidity allocation across different firms, asGreenwald et al. (2024)andChodorow-Reich et al. (2022)point out. Finally, my framework has implications beyond the scope of this study. For instance, it could shed light on credit line runs and liquidity rationing resurfacing during the COVID-19 crisis. The framework can also be applied to similar dynamic strategic complementarities in other contexts, such as fund redemption restrictions, swing pricing, and partial defaults on sovereign debt (Cipriani et al. 2014,Hanson et al. 2015,Li et al. 2021).
